description: Subscribing the same callback twice produces two independent registrations (#lzdartobservercow). `obs_x1` and `obs_x2` share the callback labelled `x`; both MUST be invoked on each notification — so callback `x` runs twice per publish — and each disposer MUST remove exactly one of them, leaving the other firing. What breaks otherwise: a binding that keys its observer collection by callback identity or callback equality collapses the two into one, and then the first caller to unsubscribe silently cancels the second caller's subscription. That is not a hypothetical — two components subscribing the same bound method is ordinary code, and neither one can see the other to defend against it. Deduplication is also simply inexpressible where callbacks are neither hashable nor comparable, which is most bindings' natural closure type, so a family that permits it cannot be implemented uniformly. Assertions are on which registration labels fired and how many invocations occurred in total; nothing here inspects the collection. Known to fail today against lazily-py (dedups by equality via a `set`) and lazily-zig (dedups by callback address, returning 'not added').

expect.note: the shared callback `x` runs twice per notification, once per registration
disposing one registration MUST leave the other — this is the clause a dedup collapses
the fully-unsubscribed callback can be registered again and behaves as a fresh registration
